title: Broken DNS Resolution
scenario: After a routine server migration, all DNS lookups have stopped working. Applications that use hostnames (like curl google.com) are failing, but the network link itself appears functional. A previous admin accidentally wrote a non-existent nameserver into /etc/resolv.conf. Your job is to isolate whether this is a DNS-layer issue or a full network outage, then fix the resolver configuration.
symptoms: dig google.com fails or times out
curl google.com fails with name resolution error
mission: Back up /etc/resolv.conf to /tmp/resolv.conf.bak. Confirm dig google.com fails while ping 8.8.8.8 succeeds (proving DNS-layer isolation). Fix /etc/resolv.conf to use a valid nameserver (8.8.8.8 or 1.1.1.1). Verify dig google.com now resolves.

hints.title: Back up before you change anything
hints.content: Run: cp /etc/resolv.conf /tmp/resolv.conf.bak — good habit: always keep a copy of what you're about to change.

hints.title: Prove it's DNS, not the network
hints.content: Run 'ping -c 3 8.8.8.8'. If pings succeed, the IP layer is fine. Then run 'dig google.com'. If dig fails but ping 8.8.8.8 works, the problem is ONLY in name resolution, not connectivity.

hints.title: Read the broken config
hints.content: Run: cat /etc/resolv.conf — look at the nameserver line. Does that IP (1.2.3.4) look like a real DNS server to you?

hints.title: Fix the resolver
hints.content: Run: echo 'nameserver 8.8.8.8' | sudo tee /etc/resolv.conf — this replaces the broken config with Google's public DNS. Use 'sudo tee' because /etc/resolv.conf is root-owned.

hints.title: Verify the fix
hints.content: Run: dig google.com — you should now see an ANSWER SECTION with IP addresses. Also try: curl -s https://google.com -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' to confirm HTTP resolution also works.
